coredata.dc:title: Magnetic molecular imprinting-restricted access materials combined with luminous boric acid carbon dots for fluorescence monitoring of Tetracycline

coredata.dc:description: In biological samples, the accurate fluorescence detection of tetracycline (TC) affects by not only the interfering molecules but also the nonspecific adsorption of marcromolecules such as proteins. This study developed magnetic molecular imprinting–restricted access materials (MMIP–RAMs) for TC detection. The MMIP–RAMs was synthesized using magnetic poly(chloromethylstyrene-co-divinylbenzene) (Fe3O4@PVBC/DVB) microspheres as the substrate, through two-step surface initiated-atom radical polymerization, TC-molecular imprinting polymers as the recognition sites and zwitterionic phosphorylcholine (MPC) polymers as the anti-protein sites were successively constructed onto Fe3O4@PVBC/DVB surface. The resulting MMIP–RAMs of Fe3O4@PVBC/DVB@MIP@poly(MPC) exhibited a high TC adsorption capacity (138.6 mg g−1) and an anti-protein capacity (above 96.8 %). Meanwhile, boric acid-containing carbon dots (BA-CDs) were prepared by citric acid and 3-aminobenzeneboronic acid precursors through hydrothermal method. In fluorescence detection, MMIP–RAMs could specially recognize TC by its imprinted cavity, after adding luminous BA-CDs, the combined MMIP–RAMs/TC could further bond BA-CDs with aid of boron affinity forces between cis-diol in TC and the boric acid groups in BA-CDs, forming MMIP–RAMs/TC/BA-CDs sandwich particles. The changes of pristine and remaining fluorescence intensity of BA-CDs had a linear relationship to TC content, establishing a simple fluorescence detection method. The fluorescence detection of TC was in the range of 44 − 264 ng mL−1 and low detection limit of 7.1 ng mL−1. When applying this method in milk, the fluorescence interference of background protein was completely eliminated by MMIP–RAMs to guarantee the accuracy of fluorescence determination, the resulting recovery rates of TC in milk were 100.0 − 105.4 % with relative standard deviations of 4.8 − 7.9 %, highlight application potential.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In biological samples, the accurate fluorescence detection of tetracycline (TC) affects by not only the interfering molecules but also the nonspecific adsorption of marcromolecules such as proteins. This study developed magnetic molecular imprinting–restricted access materials (MMIP–RAMs) for TC detection. The MMIP–RAMs was synthesized using magnetic poly(chloromethylstyrene-co-divinylbenzene) (Fe3O4@PVBC/DVB) microspheres as the substrate, through two-step surface initiated-atom radical polymerization, TC-molecular imprinting polymers as the recognition sites and zwitterionic phosphorylcholine (MPC) polymers as the anti-protein sites were successively constructed onto Fe3O4@PVBC/DVB surface. The resulting MMIP–RAMs of Fe3O4@PVBC/DVB@MIP@poly(MPC) exhibited a high TC adsorption capacity (138.6 mg g−1) and an anti-protein capacity (above 96.8 %). Meanwhile, boric acid-containing carbon dots (BA-CDs) were prepared by citric acid and 3-aminobenzeneboronic acid precursors through hydrothermal method. In fluorescence detection, MMIP–RAMs could specially recognize TC by its imprinted cavity, after adding luminous BA-CDs, the combined MMIP–RAMs/TC could further bond BA-CDs with aid of boron affinity forces between cis-diol in TC and the boric acid groups in BA-CDs, forming MMIP–RAMs/TC/BA-CDs sandwich particles. The changes of pristine and remaining fluorescence intensity of BA-CDs had a linear relationship to TC content, establishing a simple fluorescence detection method. The fluorescence detection of TC was in the range of 44 − 264 ng mL−1 and low detection limit of 7.1 ng mL−1. When applying this method in milk, the fluorescence interference of background protein was completely eliminated by MMIP–RAMs to guarantee the accuracy of fluorescence determination, the resulting recovery rates of TC in milk were 100.0 − 105.4 % with relative standard deviations of 4.8 − 7.9 %, highlight application potential.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Food safety is a substantial public-health challenge. Tetracycline (TC) is widely used in animal husbandry owing to its favorable antibacterial efficacy, low toxicity, and cost-effectiveness. However, the long-term consumption of TC in food can cause various bodily harms such as allergic reactions, hearing loss, and gastrointestinal disorders [1]. The number of food safety incidents toward TC has increased in recent years [2]. To ensure food safety, the World Health Organization, European Union, and the National Health Commission of China have established a maximum residue limit (MRL) of 100 μg kg−1 for TC in milk (GB31650–2019). In the United States, the Food and Drug Administration has set an MRL of 300 μg kg−1 for TC in raw milk. Therefore, identifying TC in animal-derived products is an imperative and pivotal task in food safety.

Various strategies for the detection of TCs have been developed and achieved fruitful results, including liquid chromatography–electrospray ionization tandem mass/mass spectrometry (LC–ESI–MS/MS), high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC), and electrochemical methodologies [3–5]. However, the existing TC detection methods are limited by the inadequate sensitivity and selectivity, the longer detection time, the requirement of expensive instruments, and the demand for technologically proficient personnel [6–8]. So far, fluorescent detection has garnered increasing attention as a simple, low cost, highly sensitive, and fast-response method, but often yields false positive results because of recognizing TC with poor selectivity.

Molecularly imprinted technology, which is specifically recognize the target molecule through the formation of molecular imprinted polymer (MIP) as the imprinting cavity to morphological compatibility with the template molecules, is widely approved for its high selectivity and sensitivity and rapid detection capabilities [9]. To date, a variety of MIP has also gained significant attention and extensive application in TC detection [10–12]. MIP is unable to directly induce a optical response signal towards analyte, frequently, MIP for fluorescence detection is fabricated by coating a layer of imprinted polymer on the surfaces of fluorescence luminous microsphere [13–15]. However, this fluorescent MIP are unsuitable for rapid detection and sensitive response because the comparatively wide distance between the target molecules and luminescent materials extends the response time [16–18].

And beyond that, when fluorescence detection is applied in the biological samples, such as food detection, disease diagnosis, environmental monitoring, etc., the existing biofouling compounds such as proteins could produce the nonspecific adsorption with fluorescent materials, inducing interference with fluorescence detection [19]. Restricted access materials (RAMs), which modify their hydrophilic surface polymers to automatically eliminate the interference of biofouling compounds such as proteins while selectively extract the targets, are classified as “intelligent” materials [20–22]. Theoretically, the excellent antifouling performance can be attributed to highly hydrophilic strength of RAMs surface, promoting the exclusion capacity to proteins. Recently, we demonstrated that grafting of high-density zwitterionic polymers by surface-initiated atom radical polymerization (SI-ATRP) are highly hydrophilic, exhibiting excellent anti-biolofouling capacity during the detection of biological samples [23,24]. Inspired by our preliminary studies, here, we propose a zwitterionic-type molecularly imprinted polymer-restricted access material (MIP–RAM) for TC detection with enhanced selectivity and low protein interference.

To sensitively and accurately determine TC by fluorescence detection in biological samples, in this study, magnetic MIP–RAMs are combined with quantum carbon dots (CDs) to integrate the high selectivity of MIP, the anti-fouling properties of RAMs, and the luminescence of CDs (Fig. 1
A and B). More specifically, magnetic poly(chloromethylstyrene-co-divinylbenzene) (Fe3O4@PVBC/DVB) microspheres were prepared as the substrate. Using methylacrylic acid (MAA) as the functional monomer and TC as the template molecule, magnetic MIPs (MMIPs) were then prepared via SI-ATRP. Subsequently, zwitterionic 2-methacryloyloxy ethyl phosphorylcholine (MPC) was block-grafted on the MMIP surfaces via the second SI-ATRP process, obtaining Fe3O4@PVBC/DVB@MIP@poly(MPC) particles (denoted as MMIP–RAMs). The adsorption capacity to TC and the anti-interference ability to protein of MMIP–RAMs were investigated in detail, demonstrating it with dual-function of capturing TC while inhibiting proteins. Meanwhile, luminous probe of boric acid-type CDs (BA-CDs), which were prepared using citric acid (CA) and 3-aminobenzeneboronic acid (3-APBA) as precursors through hydrothermal method, could specifically recognize TC with cis-diol groups by boron affinity interactions. In fluorescence detection, MMIP–RAMs and TC could be firstly the quantitative combination by specific imprinted cavity. After the first magnetic separation, the formed MMIP–RAMs/TC was added the quantitative BA-CDs suspension, the boron affinity forces between BA-CDs and cis-diol-containing TC could lead to form MMIP–RAMs/TC/BA-CDs sandwich particles. After the second magnetic separation, the fluorescence changes of pristine intensity (F 0) to the remaining intensity (F i) of BA-CDs had the relationship with the TC concentration, establishing fluorescence detection toward TC (Fig. 1 C). Our established fluorescence detection could solve the problem of the current fluorescent MIP, which are unsuitable for sensitive fluorescence response because of the comparatively wide distance between the target molecules and luminescent material. Lastly, the application potential of our fluorescence method was implemented in milk sample, highlighting MMIP–RAMs with both capacity in the selective recognizing TC while the efficient inhibiting protein interference in biological sample, to ensure the accurate determination of fluorescence intensity after BA-CDs adding.
The materials and reagents are detailed in the Supporting Information.
The test instruments are displayed in the Supporting Information.

The BA-CDs were prepared using the reported hydrothermal method with some improvements [25]. 3-APBA (1.4 g) and CA (1.9 g) were placed in a 100-mL beaker with 25.0 mL deionized water and ultrasonically dissolved (Fig. 1 A). The solution was transferred to a teflon-lined autoclave and then heated at 180 °C for 8 h. The product was dialyzed for 24 h to obtain CDs containing boric acid groups.
The MMIP–RAM materials were synthesized as shown in Fig. 1 B. The specific process is described below.

Preparation of Fe3O4@PVBC/DVB: PVBC/DVB microspheres were prepared via one-step seed swelling as described in our previous report [26]. The PVBC/DVB microspheres (2.0 g) were added along with FeCl2·4H2O (8.0 g) and FeCl3·6H2O (15.0 g) to a mixed solution of 15.0 mL methanol/water (50/50, v/v) and 4.0 mL concentrated HCl. After heating to 60 °C and quickly adding 1.5 mol L–1 NaOH solution (125.0 mL), the reaction proceeded for 30 min. Magnetic separation yielded the product Fe3O4@PVBC/DVB, then it was vacuum dried at 60 °C for 6 h.

Preparation of MMIP: Fe3O4@PVBC/DVB@MIP was synthesized via the first SI-ATRP. Fe3O4@PVBC/DVB (1.0 g), TC (0.1 g), 1.0 mL MAA (1.0 mL), ethylene glycol dimethacrylate (1.2 mL) and 2,2′-bipyridine (Bipy) (0.1 g) were successively dissolved in a flask containing 30.0 mL ACN/water solution (80/20 v/v). After three freeze–vacuum-thaw cycles, 0.3 g of CuBr was quickly added to this mixture and reacted for 6 h at 60 °C. The MMIP product, Fe3O4@PVBC/DVB@MIP, was collected by magnetic separation. Subsequently, the TC template was removed by Soxhlet extraction using glacial acetic acid–methanol (20/80, v/v) as the eluent. The elution process was continuously monitored via HPLC until no TC was detected. The MMIP product, Fe3O4@PVBC/DVB@MIP, was washed with water and dried for 12 h.
Non-imprinted MNIP (Fe3O4@PVBC/DVB@NIP) was synthesized via the same process without the TC template and Soxhlet extraction.

Grafting of MPC for synthesis of MMIP-RAMs: the MPC was grafted by the second SI-ATRP. MPC (1.6 g), Bipy (0.1 g) and Fe3O4@PVBC/DVB@MIP (or Fe3O4@PVBC/DVB@NIP) (1.0 g) were dissolved in 100 mL of a methanol/water solution (50/50 v/v). After three freeze–vacuum-thaw cycles, 0.3 g CuBr was added and the mixture was heated at 60 °C for 24 h. Magnetic separation yielded the MMIP–RAM product Fe3O4@PVBC/DVB@MIP@poly(MPC) or the MNIP–RAM product Fe3O4@PVBC/DVB@NIP@poly(MPC).

The static adsorption experiments was explored to test adsorption capacity (Q). The specific process is as follows: 10 mg of Fe3O4@PVBC/DVB@MIP@poly(MPC) or Fe3O4@PVBC/DVB@MIP were dispersed in 50 mL of different TC solution (TC, OTC, CTC, DOX) in the concentration range of 2–80 mg L-1 for 3 h at 25 ℃. After magnetic separation, the obtained solution was filtered by 0.2 μm nylon membrane to analyze by ultraviolet–visible light (UV–vis) spectrophotometry. At each concentration, the process was repeated three times and Q of the TCs was calculated by Eq. (1) according to the difference between the initial and final concentrations of TCs in the obtained solutions.
(1)
Q=(C 0–C e) V/m
where C 0 and C e (mg L-1) are the analyte concentrations before and after adsorption, respectively V (L) is the volume of solution, and m (g) is the mass of the adsorbent.

The isotherm adsorption curve was obtained by plotting the equilibrium adsorption capacity Q e against the initial TC concentration in solution. The isotherm curves of MMIP–RAMs were fitted by the Langmuir and Freundlich models (Eqs. (2) and (3), respectively):
(2)
C e/Q e =C e/Q m + 1/K L Q m
(3) lnQ e = lnC e/n + lnK F
where Q m (mg g−1) is the maximum adsorption capacity, and K L (L mg−1) and K F (mg1-n Ln g−1) are the equilibrium constants of the Langmuir and Freundlich models, respectively.

The TC adsorption rates of the materials were investigated in dynamic adsorption experiments. Several 10 mg portions of the MMIP-RAMs or MMIP were accurately weighed, dispersed in 50 mL of TC solution at a concentration of 60 mg L-1, and then shaken for 3–90 min. After magnetic separation, the obtained solution was filtered through a 0.2 μm nylon membrane for UV–vis spectrophotometer.

The kinetic adsorption curves were obtained by plotting Q e against the adsorption time. The rate-determining step (physical adsorption or chemical adsorption) was determined by fitting the curves to the pseudo-first-order (Eq. (4)) and pseudo-second-order (Eq. (5)) kinetic rate models:
(4) ln(Q e − Q t) = lnQ e − K 1 t
(5) t/Qt = 1/K2 Qe 2 + t/Qe

where Qt (mg g−1) is the adsorption capacity at t (min), and K 1 and K 2 (g mg−1∙min−1) are the pseudo-first-order and pseudo-second-order rate constants, respectively, obtained by plotting the line intercept and slope of ln (Q e − Qt ) against t and t/Qt against t, respectively.

Fluorescence detection was implemented by the combination of MMIP–RAMs with luminous BA-CDs (Fig. 1 C). In this experiment, 10 mg of the MMIP–RAM particles (Fe3O4@PVBC/DVB@MIP@poly(MPC)), were dispersed and shaken in TC solutions of different concentrations. After 5 min of adsorption, MMIP–RAMs/TC composite particles was obtained with a magnet. A quantitative BA-CDs suspension (adjusted to pH = 8.5) was then added to the MMIP–RAMs/TC, obtaining the MMIP–RAMs/TC/BA-CDs sandwich-structured particles through boron affinity forces. A second magnetic separation retrieved the uncombined BA-CDS in solution.

The fluorescence intensities of the original BA-CDs (F 0) and the remaining BA-CDS (Fi ) were recorded at excitation and emission wavelengths of 370 and 420 nm, respectively. The calculated ΔF/F0 ratio, where ΔF = F 0 − Fi , was a linear function of TC concentration.

The MMIP–RAMs (10 mg) were dispersed in mixed solutions of TC (0.2 mg mL−1) and bovine serum albumin (BSA) or lysine (Lys) (40 mg mL−1) as model proteins. After magnetic separation, 20 mL of a BA-CD solution was added to the MMIP–RAMs/TC–BSA (or Lys) complex, obtaining a mixed solution of MMIP–RAMs/TC–BSA (or Lys)/BA-CDs. The mixed solution was again separated with a magnet and the fluorescence intensity of the uncombined BA-CDs suspension was subsequently analyzed at 370 nm. Control MMIP particles, which were prepared without grafting poly(MPC), were tested by the same process to highlight the function of RAMs in fluorescence detection.

Next, the anti-protein interfering capacities of Fe3O4@PVBC/DVB, MMIP, and MMIP–RAMs for BSA and Lys were measured using the Coomassie bright blue G-250 method. The effect of pH on the anti-protein interference efficiencies was also investigated. For these tests, 10 mg of each material was dispersed in 40 mg mL−1 (10 mL) BSA or Lys solution at different pH (6.0–10.0 at 0.5-unit intervals). The suspensions were oscillated at room temperature for 30 min. Next, 2 mL of the filtrate was added to 5 mL of Coomasie bright blue G-250 and stained for 5 min. The absorbance was determined by UV–vis spectrophotometry at 595 nm. The calculation of anti-protein capacity was used Eq. (6):
(6) Anti-protein capacity% = CeVe/C0V0 × 100 %
where C 0 and C e (g·L-1) are the protein concentrations in solution before and after exclusion, respectively, V 0 and V e (L) are the protein solution volumes before and after exclusion, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of TC in milk by MMIP–RAMs/BA-CDs of fluorescence detection
ce:section.ce:para: Through a quantitative analysis at different TC concentrations in solution (44–264 ng mL−1), the limit of detection (LOD) was determined using the 3σ rule, where σ = S 0 /S with S being the slope of the calibration curve and S 0 being the standard deviation after seven blank repeated measurements.

The real sample was milk purchased from a local supermarket in Yinchuan. First, the fluorescence intensities of the initial BA-CD suspension and milk samples were determined at 370 nm. After placing 5-mL milk samples in test tubes and adding a series of standard solutions, the samples were diluted to 66, 132 and 220 ng mL−1 with deionized water and 10 mg of MMIP–RAMs were added. After magnetic separation, BA-CD solution (20 mL) was added and the pH was adjusted to 8.5. After a second magnetic separation, the fluorescence intensity of the remaining BA-CDs in solution was detected at 370 nm and the TC concentration in the milk samples was quantified using our established quantitative method.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Construction principles and characterization of the MMIP–RAMs
ce:section.ce:para: Firstly, the BA-CDs were easily synthesized through classical hydrothermal reactions (Fig. 1 A). Secondly, the substrates of the MMIP–RAMs were self-made magnetic microspheres (Fe3O4@PVBC/DVB). Since the −CH2Cl groups in Fe3O4@PVBC/DVB could use as the halogen initiator of SI-ATRP, the MIP and zwitterionic poly(MPC) were successively block-grafted onto Fe3O4@PVBC/DVB surface by block polymerization [27], forming specific TC-recognition sites within an imprinted cavity and anti-protein interference sites. During the MIP construction process, the synthesis method was optimized by varying the quantities of the functional monomer MAA and the template molecule TC as described in our previous work [28,29]. Obviously, the whole synthesis process was mature and easily implemented.

The morphologies of the synthesized particles were investigated using scamming electron microscopy (SEM). The Fe3O4@PVBC/DVB particles were regular spheres with an approximate size of 4.91 μm (Fig. 2
A). After modification with MIP and poly(MPC), the particle size enlarged to approximately 5.13 and 5.27 μm, respectively, but the original microspherical shape was retained (Fig. 2 B and C). The minimal discrepancies in the observed polydispersity indices ( Fig. S1 ) indicated a uniform particle size distribution of our synthesized materials. Transmission electron microscopy imaging confirmed round particles of Fe3O4@PVBC/DVB (Fig. 2 D). The polymer layer was noticeably thinner on the Fe3O4@PVBC/DVB@MIP surface (Fig. 2 E) than that on Fe3O4@PVBC/DVB@MIP@poly(MPC) (Fig. 2 F) after grafting of poly(MPC). Fig. 2 G and H present the nitrogen adsorption–desorption isotherms of the developed materials. The specific surface areas (pore sizes) of Fe3O4@PVBC/DVB, MMIP, and MMIP–RAMs were determined as 193 m2 g−1 (6.2 nm), 129 m2 g−1 (5.7 nm), and 100 m2 g−1 (5.2 nm), respectively. The decreasing trends could be explained by partial pore occupancy of Fe3O4@PVBC/DVB after grafting MIP and poly(MPC), confirming the successful modification. Then, the crystal structures of Fe3O4@PVBC/DVB, MMIP, and MMIP–RAMs were analyzed by X-ray diffraction (XRD) ( Fig. S2 ). The observed 2θ (30.30, 35.50, 43.20, 57.20, and 62.70) peaks were assigned to the (220), (311), (400), (511) and (440) crystal planes (JCPDS No. 82–1533). After modifying with MIP and grafting of MPC, the characteristic spinel peaks of XRD patterns of Fe3O4@PVBC/DVB@MIP and Fe3O4@PVBC/DVB@MIP@poly(MPC) remained clearly visible, with no significant shifts in peak positions, indicating that the surface functionality did not compromise the crystalline structural integrity of the pristine Fe3O4@PVBC/DVB. Moreover, the higher water-contact angle of MMIP (127.4°) than that of MMIP–RAMs (70.8°) (Fig. 2 I and J) indicated that grafting of zwitterionic MPC remarkably enhanced the hydrophilic surface of MMIP–RAMs.

The elemental compositions of synthesized particles were characterized using X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). The XPS spectrum of Fe3O4@PVBC/DVB ( Fig. S3
A) displays the main peaks of C 1 s, Cl 2p, and Fe 2p at 284.8, 197.6, and 710.5 eV, respectively. After MIP bonding, the C 1 s peak at 284.8 eV clearly increased, and the Cl 2p peak at 197.6 eV was retained to initiate the next SI-ATRP reaction. Consequently, an obvious P 2p peak at 127.1 eV was observed in the Fe3O4@PVBC/DVB@MIP@poly(MPC) spectra. The surface functional groups of those materials were then characterized using Fourier transform infrared (FT–IR) spectroscopy ( Fig. S3
B). The peaks at 670 and 780 cm−1 in the spectrum of Fe3O4@PVBC/DVB were ascribed to the bending vibrations of Fe–O bond and C–Cl groups, respectively, and the peaks at 1511 and 1601 cm−1 corresponded to the asymmetric and symmetrical stretching of benzene ring, respectively. In the FT–IR spectrum of Fe3O4@PVBC/DVB@MIP, the new adsorption peak at 1700 cm−1 belonged to the stretching vibrations of C=O in MAA monomer and the peak at 3480 cm−1 was attributed to the O–H stretching vibrations. After MPC grafting, two new absorption peaks of P–O and P=O appeared at 1069 and 1216 cm−1, respectively. By the results of the FT–IR and XPS, those combination assured the successful preparation of MMIP–RAMs.

Fig. S4 showed the thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) of Fe3O4@PVBC/DVB, Fe3O4@PVBC/DVB@MIP and Fe3O4@PVBC/DVB@MIP@poly(MPC). Clearly, the weight loss of those materials was 14.9 % below 400 °C, the same weight losses was mainly ascribed to the similar desorption of organic solvent, evaporation of water and decomposition of Fe3O4 on the surface of Fe3O4@PVBC/DVB, Fe3O4@PVBC/DVB@MIP and Fe3O4@PVBC/DVB@MIP@poly(MPC). When the temperature was higher than 400 °C, in comparison with Fe3O4@PVBC/DVB, the thermal loss of Fe3O4@PVBC/DVB@MIP increased to 12.2 % due to the modification of MIP. And the loss of Fe3O4@PVBC/DVB@MIP@poly(MPC) increased to 26.9 %, the enhanced weight loss demonstrated that poly(MPC) was grafted successfully, and occupied 14.7 % approximately.

The specific adsorption performance of MMIP–RAMs was verified on four types of tetracycline medicaments (TCs) including TC, OTC, CTC, and DOX and using non-imprinted MNIP–RAMs as control. The adsorption capacity (Q) was obtained through an isothermal absorption experiment. The isothermal adsorption curves are shown in Fig. S5
A and B. Clearly, as the TC concentration increased from 0 to 80 mg L–1, the Q values of MMIP–RAMs and MNIP–RAMs increased sharply and then rose slowly, and eventually reached equilibrium at 60 and 20 mg L–1, respectively. The calculated maximum adsorption capacity (Q m) of MMIP–RAMs for the four TCs (94.3–138.6 mg g−1) exceeded those of the MNIP–RAMs (28.2–45.4 mg g−1) by 2–3 times, and the resulting Q m with the relative standard deviation (RSD) values were all below 5.8 % (n = 3) by three independent replicate experiments ( Table S1 ). Panels C and D in Fig. S5 showed the Langmuir and Freundlich fittings to the adsorption curves of MMIP–RAMs and MNIP–RAMs, respectively. For both materials, the Langmuir model yielded higher linear correlation coefficients (R2 > 0.9817) than the Freundlich model (R2 < 0.8814), obeying the Langmuir model, which displayed MMIP–RAMs absorbed TC via the monolayer adsorption mode with the maximum adsorption capacity on uniform MMIP–RAMs surface [20,30].

The adsorption rates of TC, OTC, CTC and DOX were also studied by adsorption kinetics experiment. As shown in Fig. S6
A and B, TC adsorption increased rapidly with the prolong adsorption time, and tended to equilibrium at the approximate 30 and 20 min for MMIP–RAMs and MNIP–RAMs, respectively. The faster adsorption rate of MNIP–RAMs than that of MMIP–RAMs was mainly ascribed to the TC-recognition sites in the imprinted cavities of MMIP–RAMs, which increased the binding number of TC to MMIP–RAMs. Reasonably, the MMIP–RAMs exhibited the longer adsorption time than non-imprinted MNIP–RAMs. To elucidate the dynamic adsorption process, the acquired data were fitted by the pseudo-first-order and pseudo-second-order rate models ( Fig. S6
C and D). The pseudo-second-order equation yielded higher R2 values than the pseudo-first-order equation, confirming that the dynamic adsorption process of TC was rate-limited by chemical adsorption (i.e., hydrophobic and hydrophilic effects between the imprinted cavities and the TC template) [20,30]. And that, in both adsorption isotherm ( Fig. S5
A) and adsorption kinetic ( Fig. S6
A) experiments, the Q of TC surpassed those of other test compounds, revealing that the MMIP–RAMs more selectively adsorb TC than its analogs (OTC, CTC, and DOX).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence detection principle of combined of MMIP–RAMs and BA-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The imprinted cavity in MMIP–RAMs could specifically recognize TC while the boric acid groups in BA-CDs could interact with the cis-diol groups in TC. Based on those highly specific interactions, fluorescence detection was established by the combination of MMIP–RAMs with luminous BA-CDs (Fig. 1 C). Specifically, when the MMIP–RAM (Fe3O4@PVBC/DVB@MIP@poly(MPC)) particles were dispersed in TC solutions, they combined with TC by their imprinted cavity in Fe3O4@PVBC/DVB@MIP@poly(MPC), obtaining MMIP–RAMs/TC composite particles after magnetic separation. Then, a quantitative BA-CDs suspension (pH = 8.5) was added to the resulting MMIP–RAMs/TC composite particles, forming MMIP–RAMs/TC/BA-CDs sandwich-structured particles through boron affinity forces between the boric acid groups in BA-CDs and the cis-diol group in TC. The second magnetic separation yielded a solution of the remaining BA-CDs without bonding to TC. Here, the fluorescence detection of TC could implement by determining the fluorescence intensity of BA-CDs before and after bonding TC. Apparently, the determination was easy and simple to implementation.

To verify the feasibility of fluorescence detection, this subsection verifies the principle by which the MMIP–RAMs combine with the luminous BA-CD bodies. Fig. 3
A displays the fluorescence spectra of BA-CDs and MMIP–RAMs. Under the excitation at 370 nm, the optimal emission wavelength of BA-CDs was 420 nm. The fluorescence intensity of the BA-CDs showed no obvious changes at different pH (2–12) during consecutive usage within one week ( Fig. S7 ), confirming the excellent stability of BA-CDs. And beyond that, the fluorescence spectra of the MMIP and MNIM–RAMs particles were not overlap the BA-CD spectrum, only the luminous BA-CD carriers presented a high fluorescence intensity (curves a, b and c in Fig. 3 B). When the BA-CDs were mixed with TC, MMIP–RAMs, and MNIP–RAMs, their fluorescence intensities slightly decreased, but did not changes obviously. This subtle response suggested that the TC, MMIP–RAMs, and MNIP–RAMs minimally affected the pristine fluorescence intensity of BA-CDs (curves d, e and f in Fig. 3 B). However, when the MMIP–RAMs particles firstly combined with TC, and then added the BA-CDs suspension, forming the MMIP–RAMs/TC/BA-CDs sandwich particles (the MMIP–RAMs were well dispersed among the TC and BA-CDs, as presented in Fig. S8 ), the fluorescence intensity was reduced greatly (curve h in Fig. 3 B). Whereas for MNIP–RAMs were successively added into quantitative TC solution and BA-CDs suspension, the florescence intensity of MNIP–RAMs/TC/BA-CDs mixed system showed only a weak alteration from that of BA-CDs (curve g in Fig. 3 B). This result demonstrated the TC-imprinted cavity in MMIP–RAMs could bind TC to easily form the MMIP–RAMs/TC mixed system, and then adding the BA-CD suspension, the cis-diol-containing TC in the cavity could specially recognize boric aicd-containing BA-CDs by boron affinity force to form the MMIP–RAMs/TC/BA-CDs ternary systems, resulting a significant reduction in fluorescence intensity. In contrast, the MNIP–RAMs lacked specific recognition cavities of TC, so it could not bind to TC and BA-CDs to form ternary systems to quantitative change of fluorescence intensity.

As above, the fluorescence quenching mainly contributed to the boron affinity force between BA-CDs and TC, resulting in the formation of MMIP–RAMs/TC/BA-CDs sandwich particles. Boric aicd as a weak Lewis acid can interact with cis-diol in alkaline solutions, forming five-membered cyclic ester [31,32]. Hence, the binding capacity of BA-CDs to TC should be effect on pH to influence the fluorescence intensity of the formed MMIP–RAMs/TC/BA-CDs. To verify this expectation, the fluorescence intensity of MMIP–RAMs/TC/BA-CDs was measured in the different pH of solutions (Fig. 3 C). Indeed, at pH below 7.5, the fluorescence intensity of MMIP–RAMs/TC/BA-CDs at 370 nm has remained largely flat, no significant fluorescence quenching was observed in acidic solutions. Fluorescence quenching appeared at pH above 7.5 and was maximized at pH 8.5 for the MMIP–RAMs/TC/BA-CDs system (Fig. 3 D), being consistent with the basic conditions favor the formation of a five-membered cyclic ester between TC and the BA-CDs [33]. As illustrated in Fig. S9
A, the zeta potentials of MMIP-RAMs/TC before and after BA-CDs binding were + 4.61 mV and −8.69 mV, respectively, being consistent with the formation of a negatively charged cyclic ester. Consequently, the MMIP–RAMs/TC/BA-CDs sandwich structure was formed via the boron affinity interaction to make fluorescence quenching. Always, the inner filter effect (IFE) is applied to explain the fluorescence quenching mechanism, generally, displays an overlap region between the adsorption spectrum of adsorber and the excitation or emission spectrum of fluorephore [34]. As shown in Fig. S9
B, an overlap between the excitation spectrum of BA-CDs and the adsorption spectra of TC was observed, indicating that the IFE-based fluorescence quenching.

To quantify the relationship of fluorescence quenching with TC concentration, as shown in Fig. 3 E, the fluorescence intensities of the original BA-CDs (F 0) and the MMIP–RAMs/TC/BA-CDs (F) were recorded separately, but the change in fluorescence intensity (F/F 0) was nonlinear correlation to TC concentration (R2 = 0.5608). Note that our established fluorescence detection process as illustrated in Fig. 1 C, the pristine fluorescence intensity of BA-CDs (F 0) was initially recorded, the fluorescence intensity of the remaining BA-CDs (F i) was then measured after magnetically separation of the MMIP–RAMs/TC/BA-CDs particles. The ΔF/F0 ratio, where ΔF = F 0 – Fi represents the difference between the fluorescence intensities of the pristine and remanding BA-CDs, exhibited a good linear with the TC concentration (R2 = 0.9967) to enable accurate quantification within the range of 0–600 nmol L–1 (Fig. 3 F).

Selectivity is important for assessing the performance of fluorescent material. Here, the fluorescence selectivity was investigated using TC, various TC analogs (CL, FFC and AS bearing cis-diol groups), and various interfering substances (Ca2+ ions, Na+ ions, glucose, l-lysine, and tryptophan, which are often present in biological samples). Each compound, TC, and other test molecule (500 nmol L–1) was mixed with the MMIP–RAM or MNIP–RAM particles and the ΔF/F 0 values were obtained using our established detection method. As presented in Fig. 4 , the ΔF/F 0 of the MMIP–RAMs was remarkably higher for TC than that of the non-target compounds. In contrast, the non-imprinted MNIP–RAM particles without template-induced molecular cavities did not specifically recognize TC. As a result, no formation of the sandwich particles and the corresponding fluorescence alteration were obtained after adding the BA-CD suspension, the obtained ΔF/F 0 did not significantly differ between TC and the other interfering substances for MNIP–RAM. Evidently, the recognition ability of MMIP–RAMs primarily stemmed from the morphological memory of the imprinting sites and their specific binding affinity to TC. Hence, when the interfering compounds did not match the imprinted cavity and lacked sites to combine with the BA-CDs, making no fluorescence quenching. This finding demonstrated the exceptional anti-interference capability of MMIP–RAM, having the potential in TC detection in complex sample.

When fluorescence detection conducts in biological samples, biomacromolecule such as proteins etc., can produce non-negligible impacts on the recognition of target compounds due to electrostatic and hydrophobic interactions between the material interface and biomolecules, causing biological contamination [35,36]. Besides, in fluorescence determination, the existing proteins could influence the fluorescence intensity. In theory, a proper modifying of anti-biofouling polymers on material surface is an effective way to solve this issue in the detection of biological samples. Here, using the common BSA and Lys as test proteins and MMIP without poly(MPC) modifying as control to explore the anti-interference efficiency, materials of MMIP and MMIP-RAMs were respectively incubated in the mixture solution of protein and TC, after implementation of detection process, the results are shown in Fig. 5
A. Obviously, for MMIP particles, the fluorescence response signal of the formed MMIP/TC (mixing BSA or Lys)/BA-CDs sandwich particles (curves a and b) was significantly lower than that of MMIP/TC (without mixing proteins) /BA-CDs (curve c), indicating the existed proteins could really induce the adverse cascade of fluorescence response. Whereas for MMIP-RAMs particles in the presence or absence of proteins in the TC solution, their fluorescence signals of the formed sandwich particles generated a consistent intensity (curves d and e). The results indicated that the construction of hydrophilicity of poly(MPC) could inhibit the nonspecific adsorption of proteins. Herein, when MMIP-RAMs was immersed in the mixture solution of protein and TC, it could restrain the nonspecific adsorption protein, resulting in the no affection in MMIP-RAMs and TC adsorption. Therefore, even if the proteins existed in testing system, the formed MMIP-RAMs/TC/BA-CDs sandwich particles could well maintain, thus, protein interference in fluorescence detection could be averted.

Next, the MMIP–RAMs were immersed in 40 mg L–1 BSA or Lys solution and their anti-fouling effect was investigated by the Coomassie brilliant blue method. As shown in Fig. 5 B, the MMIP–RAMs had the higher BSA and Lys anti-protein rates (98.5 % and 96.8 %, respectively) than MMIP (76.3 % for BSA, 65.7 % for Lys) and Fe3O4@PVBC/DVB (48.5 % for BSA, 55.1 % for Lys), attesting the good efficiency of poly(MPC) in inhibiting protein adsorption. Anti-fouling adsorption mainly utilizes the electroneutral and hydrophilic interfaces to form a strong hydration layer, which reduces nonspecific-adsorption interference [37,38]. As stated in Characterization part, after grafting of MPC, MMIP-RAMs presented a obviously lower water contact angle than MMIP, indeed, modifying poly(MPC) of MMIP-RAMs had the good hydrophilic interface to restrain the protein adsorption. Besides, as shown in Fig. 5 C, the zeta potential of MMIP-RAMs illustrated a charge close to zero under the pH of 8.5. Subsequently, the effect of pH on anti-protein efficiency was also investigated, satisfactory anti-protein rates were observed at pH = 8.5 of electrical neutrality (Fig. 5 D). Accordingly, MMIP-RAMs interface had the good anti-biofouling ability to maintain the accurate fluorescence response, and this is vital to keep high detection sensitivity to TC in complex biological medium.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence detection of TC in milk using the proposed method
ce:section.ce:para:
Applying the proposed detection method, we plotted the relationship between the fluorescence ratio ΔF/F 0 by fluorescence analysis as the Y-axis and TC concentration as the X-axis. As illustrated in Fig. 6
A, the relationship equation exhibited a good linear relationship (R2 > 0.9940) in the range of 44–264 ng mL−1 with the low detection limit (LOD) of 7.1 ng mL−1. In subsequent, the application ability of our developed method for TC determination was investigated in the milk sample, clearly, the original milk sample was measured no fluorescence response (curve a in Fig. 6 B). After dispersing the MMIP–RAMs into the milk sample and performing magnetic separation, and then the quantitative BA-CD suspension was added, the measured fluorescence signal of MMIP–RAMs/milk/BA-CDs (curve b in Fig. 6 B) was almost unchanged by comparing that of the pristine BA-CDs, indicating this milk sample without TC contamination. Next, the standard addition experiment was used for recovery verification. Specifically, three TC standard solution with the different concentrations of 66, 132 and 220ng m L–1 were added to the milk samples, respectively, the obtained ΔF/F 0 through our quantified method were substitute into the fluorescence linear equation to calculate the recoveries of TC (curve c, d and e in Fig. 6 B). The resulting recoveries ranged from 100.0 % to 105.4 % with 4.8–7.9 % of RSDs (n = 3) ( Table S2 ). Besides, the reliability of our method was evaluated by intra-day and inter-day experiments, the three spikes of milk samples were measured on one day and on each of three days. The intra-day and inter-day recoveries ranged 96.4–99.6 % with 3.8–5.5 % of RSDs and 93.1–96.2 % with 5.6–7.1 % of RSDs, respectively. The acceptable recoveries and RSDs manifested that our method had the reliable reproducibility, so it is promising to apply the detection of TC.

Moreover, the detection of TC in the same milk samples was checked by the Ningxia Center for Disease Control using the national standard method of HLB extraction coupling with LC-MS/MS, having the 87.6 − 101.3 % recoveries with 2.4 − 6.5 % RSD. The results of recovery experiment showed that our method had the matched testing capabilities with national standard method toward TC. But also must admit, the national standard method should be underwent sample treatment process prior to LC-MS/MS detection, such as removing proteins at first, and then handling the tedious enrichment steps. Table 1 gives the currently reported TC detection method in milk [39–48], by comparison with other methods, it is observed that our method proves very useful due to the advantages, such as low LOD, considerably high recoveries, and a satisfactory linear range. Most importantly, our method has the simple processing steps of biological samples to reduce the test cost and manual-assistance time.

MMIP–RAMs (Fe3O4@PVBC/DVB@MIP@poly(MPC)) were constructed molecular imprinting cavity for selective recognition of TC and zwitterionic poly(MPC) for anti-protein interference using Fe3O4@PVBC/DVB as substrate. Indeed, MMIP-RAMs possessed the highly specific recognition of TC (138.6 mg g−1 of adsorption capacity) while the excellent anti-protein ability (over 96.8 % of anti-protein rate). With the help of luminous boric acid-carbon dots (BA-CDs), “MMIP-RAMs/TC/BA-CDs” sandwich particles could be formed by the imprinted cavity between MMIP-RAMs and TC as well as boron affinity interactions between TC and BA-CDs, the relationship of TC concentration to BA-CDs fluorescence intensity of the the pristine and the remaining BA-CDs was gained, establishing the ultrasensitive, highly specific, fast and easy implementation of fluorescence detection for TC. Moreover, MMIP–RAMs could effectively eliminate the fluorescence interference from proteins, ensuring accurate and stable fluorescence signals in biological samples. The proposed method could be applied to detect TC in milk sample, having the low detection limit of 7.1 ng mL−1, good linearity over a wide range of 44–264 ng mL−1, satisfactory recoveries, and reliability of intra-day and inter-day. When comparing with the existing TC monitoring approaches, our method highlighted on the superior detection accuracy, the easy operation without the tedious sample treatment. In conclusion, our method integrates the high specific selectivity of MIP, the anti-fouling properties of RAMs, and the special recognition of cis-diol of luminous BA-CDs, with the aid of simple magnetic separation, a promising fluorescence method was developed for the highly sensitive detection and easy implementation toward biological samples.
This research did not involve human or animal samples.

Yinhai Li: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis. Weilong Zhao: Software, Investigation, Methodology, Writing – original draft. Xiaofan Tang: Software, Investigation. Fei Xu: Supervision, Conceptualization. Chunmiao Bo: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization.
